that, yeah, we're talking about referrals, but this serves a mall couple purposes. Oh, yeah. Well, retention. Right. In his business, there's month-to-month continuity, there's year-to-year renewal, there's upgrade. In many businesses, the equivalent would be, again, repeat patronage. Will it cause somebody to share their purchases in a category less and be more loyal? Wrong word, but will it cause them to stick with you longer than they would otherwise? And referrals as well. So is this pastor now more or less likely to talk about Nelson to a peer, another pastor, if he is getting cool gifts and cool stuff every so often during the course of the year than if he isn't? Absolutely. If he is asked about Nelson by a peer, is he more or less likely to respond enthusiastically if he's being gifted during the course of the year? One of the things that you mentioned just in passing, I want to bring it back up, is not to do it necessarily on a, so it's not perceived as being on a regular basis. It is far better to do it that way than not to do it at all. But when you really mechanize and standardize and automate these things, you sacrifice the surprise package. So it's still fun to open a box, but I know I'm getting a box every month. And if I've been with you for more than a year, I kind of know, okay, three times a year, it's going to be a coffee cup and a jackknife and three times a year, it's going to be cookies. And so you sacrifice some of the some of the wow to it when it becomes so predictable. So if you make it appear to be randomized when it is not randomized at all, that's the key though. It's not randomized at all. It just appears to be. Yeah, if it's randomized, it doesn't happen. That's, you know, we're going to do this three times a year. Well, what are we going to do? What are we going to do? I don't know, but we'll get around to it while you get around. But when it appears to be more randomized and less predictable, then it has greater impact. Fantastic. Now, again, if you've got a question about this or anything about referral marketing, make sure that you enter it in there. So Dan, we do have some questions here from folks. You're going to love this one. This comes from Stas Ray. What if my business is online only? Which welcome gift could I give them? Well, do you want the smart answer or do you want the answer he would like? Why don't you give him the answer that he would like and then tell him the smart answer? Just do both. Right. So you are now limited, if you're going to do online delivery, you're limited to information and entertainment. Those are the two things that you can do. So you can deliver more of the same. Some version of what you are already doing that is a gift out of the blue. You can deliver more entertainment that is a gift out of the blue. Right. But candidly, there's so much less pizzazz to an online gift than an offline gift. Even kids, it's more and more this way, but even kids are not real thrilled under the Christmas tree with a digital download. And that's it. They actually kind of want to open a package. It's kind of built into us. So, yeah, and look, two generations from now, it will have probably been bred out of us because more and more is an email greeting card and a digital download and you don't expect to, you're not trained, as you say, as a little kid. So now then you're not going to open a package with three record albums in it and be all excited. And now that becomes a part of your DNA as an adult and you expect it. You won't expect that anymore. You got a code number to use at iTunes. Right. But that's another 10 or 20 years from now. So right now, the people you're selling to still grew up opening packages.
